en: How do professionals get better at what they do?
There are two views about this. You go to school, you learn, you graduate,
ko: 두 가지 관점이 있어요. 학교 가서 배우고 졸업하고,

en: and then you go out into the world and you make your way on your own.
ko: 세상에 나가서 혼자 힘으로 나아가는 거죠.

en: Now, the contrasting view comes out of sports.
ko: 반대 관점은 스포츠에서 나와. 절대 끝나지 않는다는 거지.

en: And they say, you are never done. Everybody needs a coach.
ko: 그리고 그들은 말해, 너는 절대 끝나지 않는다고. 모두가 코치가 필요해.

en: In making it on your own, you don't recognize the issues that are standing in your way,
ko: 혼자 하면 앞을 가로막는 문제를 인식하지 못하거나,

en: or if you do, you don't necessarily know how to fix them.
ko: 알아도 고치는 법을 모를 수 있거든요.

en: And the result is that somewhere along the way, you stop improving.
ko: 그 결과, 어느 순간 성장이 멈춰.

en: So I asked a former professor of mine,
and he agreed to come to my operating room and observe me.
I didn't think there would be anything much he'd have to say when we were done.
ko: 딱히 할 말이 없을 줄 알았는데,

en: Instead, he had a whole page dense with notes.
ko: 빼곡하게 메모한 종이 한 장이 있었어요.

en: He was describing what great coaches do.
ko: 훌륭한 코치가 하는 일을 보여준 거예요.

en: They are your external eyes and ears, providing a more accurate picture of your reality.
ko: 외부의 눈과 귀가 되어 현실을 더 정확히 보여주는 거죠.

en: They're recognizing the fundamentals, they're breaking your actions down,
and then helping you build them back up again.
It made me realize that the coaches were on to something profoundly important.
ko: 코치들이 정말 중요한 걸 알고 있다는 걸 깨달았어요.

en: It's not just how good you are now, I think it's how good you're going to be that really matters.
ko: 지금 얼마나 잘하느냐가 아니라 앞으로 얼마나 나아질 수 있느냐가 중요해요.
